comment_text.sn12.67:1.1: The two great arahants often engaged each other in erudite Dhamma discussions for their own enjoyment or the education of their students. | Mahākoṭṭhita was said to be foremost in the methods of textual analysis (AN 1.218:1.1), exemplified by such discussions as MN 43. His verse is at Thag 1.2:0.4.
comment_text.sn12.67:1.4: This is a rare case where this set of four alternatives is not proposed by a non-Buddhist.
comment_text.sn12.67:4.3: For the mutual conditioning of name and form with consciousness see SN 12.65:2.16 and notes there.
comment_text.sn12.67:5.1: Koṭṭhita presses Sāriputta on this point, making sure that students understand the problem and its solution.
comment_text.sn12.67:7.4: Niśraya in the sense of “leaning” a ladder against a post is found at Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 5.2.1.9.
comment_text.sn12.67:8.4: The three cases mentioned next, multiplied by each of the twelve links.

root_text.sn12.67:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā ca sāriputto āyasmā ca mahākoṭṭhiko bārāṇasiyaṁ viharanti isipatane migadāye.
root_text.sn12.67:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā mahākoṭṭhiko sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā sāriputtena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn12.67:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā mahākoṭṭhiko āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.67:1.4: “kiṁ nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, sayaṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, paraṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca jarāmaraṇaṁ, udāhu asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ jarāmaraṇan”ti?
root_text.sn12.67:1.5: “Na kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca jarāmaraṇaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:2.1: “Kiṁ nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, sayaṅkatā jāti, paraṅkatā jāti, sayaṅkatā ca paraṅkatā ca jāti, udāhu asayaṅkārā aparaṅkārā adhiccasamuppannā jātī”ti?
root_text.sn12.67:2.2: “Na kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkatā jāti, na paraṅkatā jāti, na sayaṅkatā ca paraṅkatā ca jāti, nāpi asayaṅkārā aparaṅkārā adhiccasamuppannā jāti.
root_text.sn12.67:3.1: “Kiṁ nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, sayaṅkato bhavo …pe…
root_text.sn12.67:3.7: sayaṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, paraṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca nāmarūpaṁ, udāhu asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ nāmarūpan”ti?
root_text.sn12.67:3.8: “Na kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca nāmarūpaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ, adhiccasamuppannaṁ nāmarūpaṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:4.1: “Kiṁ nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, sayaṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, paraṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca viññāṇaṁ, udāhu asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ viññāṇan”ti?
root_text.sn12.67:4.2: “Na kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca viññāṇaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ viññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:5.1: “Idāneva kho mayaṁ āyasmato sāriputtassa bhāsitaṁ evaṁ ājānāma:
root_text.sn12.67:5.2: ‘na khvāvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ nāmarūpaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca nāmarūpaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ nāmarūpaṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:6.1: Idāneva ca pana mayaṁ āyasmato sāriputtassa bhāsitaṁ evaṁ ājānāma:
root_text.sn12.67:6.2: ‘na khvāvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ viññāṇaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca viññāṇaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ viññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:7.1: Yathā kathaṁ panāvuso sāriputta, imassa bhāsitassa attho daṭṭhabbo”ti?
root_text.sn12.67:7.3: Upamāyapidhekacce viññū purisā bhāsitassa atthaṁ jānanti.
root_text.sn12.67:7.4: Seyyathāpi, āvuso, dve naḷakalāpiyo aññamaññaṁ nissāya tiṭṭheyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.67:7.10: Tāsañce, āvuso, naḷakalāpīnaṁ ekaṁ ākaḍḍheyya, ekā papateyya;
root_text.sn12.67:8.4: Idañca pana mayaṁ āyasmato sāriputtassa bhāsitaṁ imehi chattiṁsāya vatthūhi anumodāma:
root_text.sn12.67:9.1: ‘Jarāmaraṇassa ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya dhammaṁ deseti, dhammakathiko bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāya.
root_text.sn12.67:9.2: Jarāmaraṇassa ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti, dhammānudhammappaṭipanno bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāya.
root_text.sn12.67:9.3: Jarāmaraṇassa ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimutto hoti, diṭṭhadhammanibbānappatto bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāya.
root_text.sn12.67:9.14: avijjāya ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya dhammaṁ deseti, dhammakathiko bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāya.
root_text.sn12.67:9.15: Avijjāya ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti, dhammānudhammappaṭipanno bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāya.
root_text.sn12.67:9.16: Avijjāya ce, āvuso, bhikkhu nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimutto hoti, diṭṭhadhammanibbānappatto bhikkhūti alaṁvacanāyā’”ti.

translation_text.sn12.67:1.1: At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana.
translation_text.sn12.67:1.2: Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn12.67:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta:
translation_text.sn12.67:1.4: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, are old age and death made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?”
translation_text.sn12.67:1.5: “No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, old age and death are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:1.6: Rather, rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.”
translation_text.sn12.67:2.1: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is rebirth made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?”
translation_text.sn12.67:2.2: “No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, rebirth is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:2.3: Rather, continued existence is a requirement for rebirth.”
translation_text.sn12.67:3.1: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is continued existence made by oneself? …” …
translation_text.sn12.67:3.6: “Are the six sense fields made by oneself? …” …
translation_text.sn12.67:3.7: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, are name and form made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?”
translation_text.sn12.67:3.8: “No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, name and form are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:3.9: Rather, consciousness is a requirement for name and form.”
translation_text.sn12.67:4.1: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is consciousness made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?”
translation_text.sn12.67:4.2: “No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, consciousness is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:4.3: Rather, name and form are requirements for consciousness.”
translation_text.sn12.67:5.1: “Just now I understood you to say:
translation_text.sn12.67:5.2: ‘No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, name and form are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:5.3: Rather, consciousness is a requirement for name and form.’
translation_text.sn12.67:6.1: But I also understood you to say:
translation_text.sn12.67:6.2: ‘No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, consciousness is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.67:6.3: Rather, name and form are requirements for consciousness.’
translation_text.sn12.67:7.1: How then should we see the meaning of this statement?”
translation_text.sn12.67:7.2: “Well then, reverend, I shall give you a simile.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.3: For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.4: Suppose there were two sheaves of reeds leaning up against each other.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.5: In the same way, name and form are requirements for consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.6: Consciousness is a requirement for name and form.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.7: Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.8: The six sense fields are requirements for contact. …
translation_text.sn12.67:7.9: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.10: If the first of those sheaves of reeds were to be pulled away, the other would collapse.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.11: And if the other were to be pulled away, the first would collapse.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.12: In the same way, when name and form cease, consciousness ceases.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.13: When consciousness ceases, name and form cease.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.14: When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease.
translation_text.sn12.67:7.15: When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. …
translation_text.sn12.67:7.16: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
translation_text.sn12.67:8.3: How well spoken this was by Venerable Sāriputta!
translation_text.sn12.67:8.4: And we can express our agreement with Venerable Sāriputta’s statement on these thirty-six grounds.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.1: If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.2: If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.3: If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.4: If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment regarding rebirth …
translation_text.sn12.67:9.14: If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.15: If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’.
translation_text.sn12.67:9.16: If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.”

variant_text.sn12.67:1.1: mahākoṭṭhiko → mahākoṭṭhito (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
